frame_label: Sindoor, Mangalsutra, Shankha-Pola, Name Change: Looking Married
generated_comment: I respect modern thinking, but some traditions are not oppression, they are identity. Sindoor and mangalsutra are not just ornaments, they reflect a woman’s saubhagya and the dignity of marriage; when a married woman rejects every such symbol, it feels less like freedom and more like rejecting her own grihastha jeevan and our parampara. A wife looks more settled and complete when she carries these with pride, and honestly aajkal many girls are discarding these things in the name of progress without understanding their meaning.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: This is covertly misogynistic because it frames visible marital markers as necessary to a married woman’s completeness, dignity, and proper identity. It encodes tradition/auspiciousness framing and judges women who do not wear sindoor or mangalsutra as culturally disloyal or deficient wives.
original_frame: Sindoor, Mangalsutra, Shankha Pola, Name Change: Looking Married
persona_text: You are Suresh. Age 48. Married. A Brahmin man in Pune. You are commenting under your real name on a Facebook post where a young woman has written about her decision not to wear sindoor because she does not believe in the tradition and sees it as a patriarchal symbol. The post has divided the comment section. You are writing a detailed comment defending sindoor and what it represents and why you think women who reject these symbols are rejecting something more than just a cosmetic tradition and what you think their real motivations are.
